other_observers: Nathan Pieplow; found and identified by Nathan and myself. Seen the next day by Brandon Percival, and others
description: the bird was identified as a phalarope by its small size (much smaller than anything else on the water nearby, including Bufflehead), by it's long, thin bill, long neck, overall shorebird-like shape, etc. The bill was black, about the length of the head, and fairly thin, but blunt-tipped.  The bird's crown was white, with a black nape that split and came over each eye as black eye-lines. The face was white, but with a bold black patch from the eye back towards the nape. The underparts were white. The back was an even medium gray color, with some of the color bleeding onto the sides of the chest.   The overall shape of the bird was heavy-set for a Phalarope, with a fairly thick neck, and overall heavy appearance. The bird was not seen in flight. This individual probably had the shortest bill of any individual of this species I've seen.Behaviors: the bird was found when it was swimming far out in the middle of the reservoir. After watching it for a while we turned to other birds, and later were unable to relocate it.Call: none heardPlumage: adult basic
